intro: Musically, it is characterized by the sampling of 1970s Disco and 1980s Japanese City Pop, sped up and layered with heavy drums and filters in the style of French House. Visually, the aesthetic is defined by the appropriation of vintage anime loops (specifically from the 1980s and 90s) which are recontextualized through high-saturation color grading, rhythmic editing, and neon typography to match the kinetic energy of the music.[2]

infobox.key_motifs: Looped anime GIFs, VHS degradation, neon typography, Japanese bubble economy imagery, disco lights, spinning vinyl, cassette tapes
infobox.key_colours: Neon pink, cyan, violet, magenta, yellow
infobox.related_media: Urusei Yatsura, Sailor Moon, Kimagure Orange Road, Interstella 5555

Characteristics: Pitch-shifted: Sped up to a tempo of 115–130 BPM.
Filtered: Processed with high-pass and low-pass filters to create dynamic "sweeps."
Groove-locked: Layered with heavy, side-chained kick drums and compression to create a pumping, dance-floor rhythm.
Artists: Macross 82-99: A Mexican producer who pioneered the use of City Pop samples and Sailor Moon imagery (e.g., the Sailorwave albums).
Saint Pepsi (Skylar Spence): Credited with defining the genre's groove with the album Hit Vibes (2013).
Yung Bae: Known for bringing the genre to a mainstream audience and incorporating original instrumentation.
Night Tempo: A Korean producer known for his archival approach to City Pop and official remixes of Showa-era idols.
Moe Shop: A producer who blends Future Funk production with original French and Japanese vocals, bridging the gap to Kawaii Future Bass.
Desired: Known for high-tempo energetic tracks often associated with the neon city aesthetic.
Flamingosis: Blends Future Funk with hip-hop and beatboxing influences.
Songs: "Selfish High Heels" by Yung Bae (feat. Macross 82-99)
"You Look So Good" by Moe Shop
"Baby, I'm Yours" by Breakbot (cited as an influence on the genre's sound)

name: Future Funk
